paper_title: Pair-Supervised Regularization for Selective Counterfactual Edits in Frozen Vision SAEs
email_title: Pair-Supervised Regularization for Selective Counterfactual Edits in Frozen Vision SAEs

sections.summary: This paper investigates whether pair-supervised regularization can improve the selectivity of counterfactual edits in sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on frozen DINOv2 vision features. The authors propose an SAE objective augmented with pair-conditioned losses—difference concentration, signed-direction alignment, and cross-factor separation—and an audit-gated nuisance invariance term that is only enabled if augmentations preserve factors in frozen feature space. On Shapes3D with exact single-factor counterfactual pairs and two standardized edit rules, the main result is negative: the invariance audit fails and the pair-regularized model does not outperform matched vanilla and archetypal SAEs on primary intervention metrics; a one-seed MP-SAE baseline further dominates the reconstruction–preservation trade-off.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
The paper isolates a clear, narrow hypothesis—whether pairwise supervision improves edit selectivity in a frozen-feature SAE setting—avoiding overly broad or qualitative claims.
The “audit-gated” nuisance invariance is a principled methodological contribution: nuisance invariance is only activated when empirically validated in the frozen feature space, preventing overclaims.
The proposed pairwise losses (concentration of counterfactual differences, alignment to per-factor signed centroids, and cross-factor separation) are well-motivated and simple to integrate into standard SAE training.


Experimental rigor and validation
A controlled protocol is used: same backbone (DINOv2 ViT-S/14), same representation selection procedure, matched SAE width and sparsity budgets, uniform checkpoint selection rule, and two shared edit rules.
Multiple seeds (three) are used for the main methods, and bootstrap confidence intervals are reported for key test metrics.
The nuisance audit is transparently reported (including failure to pass the 99% threshold), appropriately constraining the scope of claims.


Clarity of presentation
The paper is candid about negative results and limitations (e.g., invariance disabled after audit; active-set cap replaces pure L1 to hit budget windows).
Metrics, edit rules, and training details are clearly defined; tables are easy to interpret and are consistent with narrative claims.


Significance of contributions
As a negative result with a careful experimental setup, the paper provides a valuable corrective to assumptions that simple pair supervision will improve selective editing in frozen-feature SAEs.
The audit-gated approach and standardized evaluation pipeline contribute a reproducible template for future claims in this area.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
The nuisance-invariance component, a core conceptual piece, is never activated due to audit failure; the central method is effectively reduced to concentration/alignment/separation, limiting novelty and insight about the full proposal.
The choice of a stringent 99% factor-agreement threshold (while justifiable) may be overly conservative; no sensitivity analysis is provided to assess whether invariance activation under slightly relaxed criteria might change conclusions.
The dynamics of the moving signed centroids (μk) and their stability are not deeply analyzed; potential drift or imbalance effects across factors are not examined.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
The study is scoped to a single dataset (Shapes3D) and one frozen backbone, raising questions about generality; the CelebA transfer is not included in this rerun.
The strongest baseline (MP-SAE) is only reported with a single seed, limiting the strength of that comparison.
Representation selection favors mean-pooled patches on Shapes3D; additional representations, layers, or widths are not explored beyond the small pilot.
The top-K choice (K=8) and α grid for editing are fixed and may bias against methods that change latent geometry; sensitivity studies are limited.


Clarity or presentation issues
Some hyperparameter details (e.g., running-average behavior and update rate of μk, exact λ values—some symbols are garbled in text) could be clearer to ease replication.
Factor-wise breakdowns (per-factor edit success/off-target effects) are not provided; this could reveal cases where pair-regularization helps specific factors even if not on aggregate.


Missing related work or comparisons
While related vision SAE and concept-grounded works are cited, connections to recent paired/counterfactual pretraining paradigms (e.g., counterfactual contrastive learning) and control-relevant invariance (e.g., bisimulation-style invariance on frozen encoders) could be more explicitly discussed to situate the negative result.
Comparisons to other edit-direction estimation strategies (e.g., per-sample directions, learned per-factor latent subspaces) are not explored.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The pairwise losses are well-posed: the concentration measure C(δ) is a Herfindahl-like index on absolute latent deltas; the alignment term encourages repeatable signed directions per factor; and the separation term discourages cross-factor centroid collapse via penalized cosine similarity.
The audit-gated invariance term is methodologically sound and a strong design choice: it aligns the loss with what the frozen features actually encode, preventing invalid training signals.
Using an explicit active-set cap to match sparsity budgets ensures fair comparisons but slightly shifts the training regime away from pure L1 control; the paper is transparent about this trade-off.
The two edit rules—sparse association (top-8) and dense centroid—are reasonable and probe whether the negative result is a byproduct of the sparse heuristic; similar rankings under both rules strengthen the conclusion.


Experimental evaluation assessment
The Shapes3D setup is coherent, leveraging exact counterfactuals; train/val/test splits and pairing protocol are appropriate.
The audit results are informative: even “strict photometric” augmentations fail worst-factor agreement (0.756), underscoring the need to validate nuisance assumptions for frozen encoders.
The main results are internally consistent: pair regularization marginally raises concentration but does not improve target success, off-target preservation, or selective score compared to vanilla/Archetypal SAEs; differences fall within overlapping bootstrap CIs.
Ablations at budget B (seed 11) further indicate that removing pair losses can improve selective score, suggesting the added structure may not align with the intervention objective in this frozen-feature regime.
The MP-SAE baseline shows a compelling reconstruction–preservation advantage, but with only one seed; extending it to three seeds would strengthen the conclusion that sparse-coding family dominates the trade-off.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
DINOv2 (Oquab et al., 2024) is the chosen backbone; the paper’s invariance audit resonates with findings that pretrained features may not guarantee nuisance invariance in downstream objectives.
Counterfactual contrastive learning (CF-CL, 2409.10365) finds benefits from paired counterfactuals in representation learning; here, however, pair supervision is applied at the SAE stage atop frozen features, not during backbone pretraining. The contrast highlights that where and how pairing is introduced matters—pairing at the SAE layer did not improve selective edits in this setup.
Bisimulation-style invariance learning on frozen encoders (2602.18639) emphasizes verifying/enforcing control-relevant invariances; this paper’s audit-gated approach is aligned in spirit, and its negative audit result reinforces the need for explicit validation rather than assuming invariances.
Supervised/grounded SAE works (e.g., Concept-SAE, SPARC) demonstrate that supervision can aid targeted interventions; the present study narrows the claim space by showing that pair labels, without concept masks/labels and with frozen features, do not necessarily improve selective editing.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
This is a careful negative result: it tempers expectations that modest pairwise supervision will improve selectivity in frozen-feature SAEs and underscores that stronger sparse-coding choices (e.g., MP-SAE) may be more impactful.
The audit-gated nuisance validation provides a useful template for the community, discouraging unvalidated invariance claims.
While scoped to a synthetic dataset and a single backbone/representation, the protocol and findings will be valuable for researchers designing supervised SAE objectives and evaluation pipelines.
sections.questions_for_authors:
How sensitive are conclusions to the 99% nuisance-agreement threshold? Would an exploratory analysis at 95–98% (clearly labeled as such) help assess whether invariance activation changes any trends?
How are the running signed centroids μk updated (explicit EMA rate, debiasing), and how stable are they across epochs and seeds? Any evidence of centroid drift or dominance by a few high-δ samples?
Could you provide per-factor breakdowns (target success, off-target preservation) to check whether pair regularization benefits specific factors even if aggregate metrics are unchanged?
How sensitive are the results to the top-K choice (K=8) and the α grid? Did larger/smaller K or factor-specific α values change the relative ranking?
Did you experiment with per-sample or conditional edit directions (e.g., local Jacobian-based or nearest-neighbor centroids) rather than global μk? If so, how did they compare?
What were the exact λ values for concentration/alignment/separation (some symbols appear garbled)? Could you share a brief hyperparameter sensitivity plot for λc, λa, and λo?
How robust are results to SAE width and to alternative layers/representations (e.g., earlier token layers or CLS vs. mean-pooled patches) beyond the small pilot?
Can you extend MP-SAE to three seeds to solidify the claim that sparse-coding family dominates the trade-off?
Would a synthetic, trivially validated nuisance family (e.g., a carefully constructed transformation known to preserve factors in feature space) allow you to test the invariance term in at least a toy setting?
Is code and the audit/evaluation pipeline planned for release to facilitate replication and further negative/positive studies?
Did you analyze correlations between the concentration metric C(δ) and selective score across methods/seeds? The weak link may indicate C(δ) is not a good proxy—any insights?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    5/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            This paper offers a careful, well-scoped negative result on a timely interpretability question: whether pairwise supervision can improve the selectivity of counterfactual edits in frozen vision SAEs. The methodology—including an audit-gated invariance mechanism, matched sparsity budgets, and shared edit rules—is thoughtfully designed and transparently reported. Although the full objective is partially untested due to failed nuisance audits, the study still yields a clear take-away: modest pair regularization (concentration/alignment/separation) does not outperform strong or simpler SAE baselines in this frozen-feature regime on Shapes3D, and sparse-coding family choices likely matter more. The work’s value lies in its rigorous evaluation protocol and its candid narrowing of claims for supervised SAEs. However, the limited scope (single dataset/backbone, invariance disabled, single-seed MP-SAE) and modest originality of the loss components constrain its suitability for a top-tier venue. I view this as a solid, informative negative result that would benefit the community—particularly if accompanied by code and expanded baselines—but it falls short on breadth and novelty for ICLR acceptance in its current form.
